meta.title: Restraint Glove for Disabled Elderly People
meta.description: Within necessary restraint, provide comfort, reassurance, and autonomy. This is a long-term design and implementation project centered on restraint gloves for disabled elderly people.
meta.summary: Within necessary restraint, provide comfort, reassurance, and autonomy.
meta.breadcrumb: Restraint Glove for Disabled Elderly People

sections.title: Project Overview
sections.headline: Restraint Glove for Disabled Elderly People
paragraphs: This is a design project about end-of-life care. It ran through the final two years of my high-school design coursework, and it was also the main reason I chose to take a gap year after senior year before going to university.
What I want to make is not just a ‘better-looking restraint tool,’ but this: when restraint is unavoidable, how can comfort, reassurance, and autonomy be returned to the person receiving care?
sections.quote: How much pain must a person seek relief from through another kind of pain?

image.alt: Prototype of the restraint glove for disabled elderly people

stats.title: Key Progress
items: Background research: completed
Patent strategy: Chinese utility model application in progress
Product iteration: V3 completed
Expert consultation: related nursing staff from 2 care facilities; Professors Minki and Rudi at Shanghai NYU
Pitching and fundraising: completed

stats.title: Timeline
items: Small-batch production (non-medical device): in progress (April–June 2026)
Product launch: July 2026
Medical filing: depends on market response (2027)
Product line expansion: depends on market response (2028)

sections.title: What Prompted This
paragraphs: In my second year of high school, the large iron gate of our residential compound injured my grandmother. After joint replacement surgery, she moved into a care home. I went back on weekends to stay with her, and spent one night there. Four beds, four elderly residents, and a caregiver sleeping on a makeshift bed by the door. Why by the door? Because if someone became disoriented and tried to run out in the middle of the night, someone had to notice immediately to prevent them from getting lost or falling.

That day, my grandmother was in good spirits. During rehabilitation she even walked an extra lap with me. She fell asleep early that night, but I kept waking because I am a light sleeper. In the middle of the night, I heard murmuring and the sound of bed rails being struck. I turned and saw the elderly woman in the next bed rocking back and forth, and the one beyond her swinging her hands against the rails. That was when I noticed their hands were restrained.

The next day, in daylight, I saw clearly that they were wearing two pairs of blue restraint gloves. The caregiver told me: the woman on the left, Xiaochun, always wanted to go home and kept trying to run out; the woman on the right, Xiaocao, had paralysis in her lower body, recurring old injuries, and great difficulty communicating. The gloves provided buffering, but they did not answer another question: what were they actually enduring at night? Was there another possibility? I decided to begin my investigation there, and that also became the start of the companionship and observation that followed.

note_image.alt: Screenshot of notes from the starting point of the project
note_image.caption: An early project record. It feels more like a starting point: older people are so often forgotten by the world, and human time does not wait for ‘later.’

sections.title: Background Research
sections.intro: Restraint is a tool frequently used in real caregiving. It usually appears in situations involving disability, fluctuating consciousness, or risks such as falls, self-scratching, self-injury, or accidental tube removal. Without restraint, some risks occur; with restraint, new harms can appear as well.

risk_columns.title: Without restraint, risks may come from
items: Falls
Self-scratching and self-injury
Accidental tube removal
Other caregiving adverse events

risk_columns.title: With restraint, new harms may come from
items: Agitation and psychological distress
Reduced range of movement
Poor fit for contracted hands
Severe adverse events, including finger necrosis and fatal suffocation

case_box.title: One case I could not ignore
case_box.text: In 2022, there was a publicly documented case in China: an elderly person’s hand became stuck and necrotic inside the mesh because of a ‘finger restraint ring,’ and it was not discovered until a week later. Care protocol originally required ‘removing the glove every 2 hours to check the hand,’ but in a high-intensity real care environment, that is not easy to do consistently. Design should not push all key risks onto the user; it should reduce blind spots structurally wherever possible—for example, by making the condition of the hand visible.

subsection.title: The prevalence of restraint and long-term bedridden care
paragraphs: The care home I visited follows a principle of ‘minimal restraint.’ Even so, 39% of the elderly residents there still wore restraint gloves; among those who wore restraint gloves, 92% were also long-term bedridden. In the facility overall, 53% of residents were long-term bedridden, and 68% of that group wore restraint gloves.

These numbers show that in the actual care-home environment I entered, restraint gloves were not a rare situation but a fairly common care reality. It does not stop existing just because we choose not to look at it.

sections.title: Care Log
intro_paragraphs: When I returned to the care home that week, my grandmother had already been discharged. The new grandmother in the bed was a hearing-impaired woman with smiling eyes; the ones still under restraint were Xiaochun and Xiaocao. I helped feed Xiaocao liquid food. I was clumsy at it, but I encouraged her bite by bite: ‘Eat a little more, your body will have a bit more strength.’ She nodded hard and replied, ‘Okay, okay, okay.’

After the meal I wiped her mouth and tried chatting with her. She repeated short words, but she was entirely understandable. I heard her say ‘good, good,’ and I heard her say ‘this hurts, this hurts.’ I pressed gently near her arm and asked whether it hurt there; at almost every spot she said yes. Sometimes she tried to lift her hand to scratch her face, and the edge of the blue glove scraped against her skin. I took a tissue and gently touched her cheek for her. She suddenly said: ‘You’re so kind.’
In that moment, I thought: she was not confused at all.

cards.title: Xiaocao
paragraphs: The caregiver told me her family had long since moved abroad, and that very few people spent this much time with her. The sentence she said most often was ‘Don’t go.’ As long as I stood by her, her eyes regained some light and the struggling of her hands eased. The moment we left for lunch break or work, she returned to murmuring at the ceiling, groaning, and striking things. The glove could buffer the impact, but it could not buffer what she was truly trying to say, or what she was truly enduring.
cards.quote: How much pain must a person seek relief from through another kind of pain?

cards.title: Xiaochun
paragraphs: The first time Xiaochun met me, she mistook me for a thief and yelled at me to get out; the second time, she warmly held my hand and introduced the buildings outside the window. Her family came several times a week, bringing her food, helping her wash up, and keeping her company. But in the middle of the night she always wanted to run out. A family can stop it once, twice—but not for a month, or a whole year. Restraint was not the outcome they wanted, but it became a reality they had to accept.

She was strong during the day: she joined rehabilitation exercises, wrestled with the numbers on the machine, and would suddenly want to move a stool or go to the window to look outside. The sunlight was good, and she still had strength. Precisely because she still had that strength, the possibility of nighttime loss of control, falls, and wandering was all the more real.

sections.title: Product Research
sections.intro: After returning to school, I began systematically studying existing restraint products. The most common ‘blue glove’ can satisfy the most basic restraint function, but from both the elderly user’s perspective and the nurse’s, it is sparse. Another kind of glove tries to include more human-centered care through squeeze components, but it fills the hand space completely, and when paired with rough mesh the experience actually becomes worse. There are also hard-shell products that emphasize 360-degree protection. Their structure is more complete, but the knocking noise and rigid feel make them difficult for many elderly people to accept.

cards.title: Basic blue glove
cards.text: It satisfies the basic restraint function, but offers little improvement in comfort or user experience.

cards.title: Glove with squeeze components
cards.text: It tries to add a soothing function, but can easily take over the hand’s space, and rough materials can cancel out the original good intention.

cards.title: Hard-shell glove
cards.text: Protection is more complete, but knocking noise and rigid fit create new discomfort.

sections.closing: Some of the design details that truly matter are not the sort of functions you notice at first glance, but the places whose value only appears in real use. For example, mesh that is both visible and sturdy enough: it allows caregivers to check the condition of the elderly person’s hand at any time, and it also preserves the person’s basic visual awareness of their own hand, encouraging movement instead of making the hand disappear into an unseen space.

versions.title: V1 (2024 - 2025)
versions.intro: This stage did not yet have practical functionality. The focus was to first build out three directions together: a glove body with soft squeeze structures, an airbag for different relaxed arm positions, and an electronic button for interaction to ease loneliness.
items: When elderly users touched the soft squeeze module, they would naturally rub it without any explanation.
Some users felt pain where the base plate pressed against the ulnar styloid at the wrist.
One caregiver suggested: since there is already an airbag, why not develop it further into a massage device?
Family members recognized the direction of the button feature, but also asked: if an elderly person can operate a button, do they still need restraint?

versions.title: V2 (2025 - 2026)
versions.tag: Focused on the minimum viable product
versions.intro: This stage began to focus on making the glove body itself viable. The emphasis was moving the product from concept toward production: squeeze blocks of different sizes and materials, detachable wrist-pad cushioning, and structural adjustments closer to real caregiving needs.
items: For severely contracted hands, an ergonomic molded design is less effective than a simple self-adapting foam ball.
For elderly people who still want to move but have severe contracture, a long narrow rubbing-strip module can be designed.
The wrist pad is a good design, but not as important as I first imagined.
An overlong base plate, separate left-right glove shapes, and overly heavy structures all increase production cost and usage burden.
A clinical access window for the fingers still needs to be added.

versions.title: V3 (2026)
versions.intro: V3 continued refining the glove itself, while turning the massage module and button module back into basic functional prototypes: adjusting size, adding finger access windows, unifying the base plate, adding a detachable opening, and testing squeeze balls and rubbing strips of different softness.
items: The glove size still needs fine adjustment: a smaller body, narrower and longer wrist fabric, and a tighter restraint range.
The fabric needs to be puffier and thicker to provide protection, but it cannot become stiff.
More detachable functions are not necessarily better; over-fixing can instead restrict squeeze blocks that should remain free to move.
Squeeze balls and rubbing strips are also useful for elderly people who are not wearing restraint gloves; tactile stimulation can redirect attention and ease discomfort.
The care facility emphasized that the material must absorb sweat and be easy to clean.

The button module revealed a real use case: it is useful not only for some restrained elderly people who can still press buttons, but also for elderly people without restraint who cannot manage a phone with fine motor control.

sections.title: Thinking Log
paragraphs: What kind of world do elderly people live in, really? The pain of body and mind today may be the most comfortable moment they will have in the coming year; and when someone who once raised others becomes the disabled person who now needs care, where does the meaning of the time that remains go? Even if design could ease every physical pain, the emptiness and loss within human consciousness cannot be filled by design itself; and among restrained elderly people, too many are still feeling everything with full clarity.

That is also why I placed a Bluetooth button above all the other design elements: if it can connect to a phone app, TV, projector, or computer, then it is not just a ‘functional accessory.’ It lets a person retain a little autonomy even under restraint—making a call, looking at photos, changing the channel. Even a very small action still means: I can decide some things.

Human autonomy matters too much, even if it is only a small soft block that can be kneaded. A soft piece stuffed with a foam ball does not need complex design, but what it offers is not a gimmick. It is a choice that has been left in place. I also wondered whether I should first go to university, work for a few years, and then come back to push this forward from a more mature position. But as of today, two elderly people in families willing to buy this design have already passed away. Time never waits, especially once another possibility has already appeared.
sections.quote: I think this is something I can keep doing for many, many years.

blocks.title: I. Core Strategy
items: First take a ‘non-medical’ route to validate the market, using a small batch to test real purchase intent.
Do not depend on pitching results. Whether or not I receive funding, I will use my own money to start the first production run.
The goal at this stage is not to make money, but to validate the market while trying not to lose money.

cards.title: II. Product Positioning
items: Restraint glove: positioned as a ‘care support product,’ not a medical device.
Functional descriptions focus on anti-scratching and comfortable care, without claiming medical effects.
Squeeze modules can be developed as standalone soothing pads, or paired with the glove.

cards.title: III. First Production Plan
items: Basic glove: 60 units (30 pairs)
Squeeze modules: 100 units (50 pairs)
Reserved gifts / samples: 12 gloves, 18 squeeze pieces
Interview samples: 6 gloves, 6 squeeze pieces
Total first-batch investment: up to RMB 10,000, covered by my personal funds

blocks.title: IV. Sales and Validation Path
items: Open a Taobao store, set specific prices based on cost, and sell the squeeze module separately if needed.
Publish interviews and product stories on Xiaohongshu first, to attract users in non-medical scenarios.
First-batch buyers, sample recipients, and interviewees become ‘permanent VIPs’ and will receive long-term discounts in the future.
If the first batch sells out, the second batch will use a group-order production model: production starts again only after orders reach a certain number.

stages.title: Stage 1: Before production
stages.text: Bring the latest prototype back to the care home where interviews have been ongoing, let elderly residents try it, ask caregivers and family members to comment, and use that feedback to finalize the drawings. One unexpected gain is that a retirement facility in a non-medical setting has already expressed interest in a trial order.

stages.title: Stage 2: After production
stages.text: Use sampled products to gather user-experience feedback, continue interviewing caregivers and family members, create publicity materials, and leave the products with participants as thanks.

compare.title: Elderly residents suitable for interviews
items: Relatively clear consciousness, able to express feelings
Emotionally more stable after wearing it
Family members present and willing to talk
Clear difficulties with existing restraint gloves

compare.title: Elderly residents not suitable for the first round of interviews
items: Severe mania and unable to communicate
Extremely resistant to any form of restraint
Family members absent, or emotionally indifferent
Previously had a very bad experience, and the family’s anger has not yet settled

blocks.title: VI. Risk Prevention and Long-Term Direction
items: Use biocompatible fabric and non-toxic filling particles, and retain the relevant testing reports.
Clearly label it as ‘care support product, not a medical device’ and ‘must be used under supervision.’
Do not claim medical effects or promise treatment functions.
Process transactions through the Taobao store and retain sales records and receipts.

If feedback from family users is good, continue expanding production and developing a line of small caregiving products; if care facilities begin ordering on their own, then consider filing and launching a medical version.

blocks.title: VII. External Support I Need

support_cards.title: Direction and guidance
support_cards.text: Help me make decisions more clearly on top of the judgment I already have.

support_cards.title: Manufacturer
support_cards.text: A reliable factory that can support long-term collaboration.

support_cards.title: Medical contacts / resource links
support_cards.text: CROs, medical-device factories, filing consultation channels, care-facility purchasing paths, and industry expert resources.

support_cards.title: Endorsement
support_cards.text: Stand behind me and help others see that this is a project of real quality.

blocks.closing: But whether or not I receive that support, I will still use my own funds to make the first batch and let the market answer first.

sections.title: Acknowledgments
items: The elderly people, family members, and caregivers who were willing to listen and give feedback.
Teachers Pranjal, Shruti, and Jun, who supported, witnessed, and guided this whole project.
Teacher Jiangjiang, who taught me modeling, concept development, and drawing.
Teacher Alex, who taught me how to sort out the logic of layout in project presentation.
Volunteer partner Liang Yue, who listened to me share, recommended things, and supported me.
Recommended by Liang Yue, Teacher Lin Lan, who taught me about the ‘minimum viable product’ and showed me a direction.
Professor Rudi, Minki, and his team, who gave me practical professional perspective.
The people who listened to me talk about the project and shared support, warmth, and their own stories.
My partner Dongyuan, who from the beginning until now has always listened, discussed, and supported me.
